title: why i am finally making my website
blurb: a someday project for years. two things changed: AI made the build cheap, and I wanted a real excuse to learn Go.
body:
For years my personal website was a someday project. The kind of thing I would scope out, feel the size of, and quietly close the tab.

## what changed

Two things. First, I can move a lot faster now — with AI doing the heavy lifting on the frontend, the React portfolio you are reading went from *someday* to *this weekend*. The parts that used to eat evenings — layout, tokens, the fiddly responsive work — stopped being the reason not to start.

> The site stopped being a wall and started being an afternoon.

Second, I wanted an excuse to learn **Go**. Not a tutorial-shaped excuse — a real one, with routes and JSON shapes and edge cases I would have to actually handle.

## the split

So I drew a line down the middle of the repo:

- The **frontend**, docs, and tooling are AI-generated — directed and reviewed by me, but not hand-typed.
- The **backend** — the Go API — is mine. Handwritten, on purpose. Writing it *is* the point; letting a model do it would defeat the exercise.

```
frontend/   # AI-built React + a mock API stand-in
backend/    # handwritten Go — the whole reason
```

Right now a small Express server mocks the API so the site has something real to fetch while the Go service grows up next to it. Same routes, same JSON, different author.

## why finally

Because the tradeoff finally made sense: let the machine build the part I have built a hundred times, and spend my own hours on the part I actually want to learn.
